text: The surgeon evaluates the transabdominal anatomy, revealing a large indirect inguinal hernia without involvement of visceral organs.

The surgeon develops a peritoneal flap starting high around the arcuate line, working towards the midline near the pubis. The lateral dissection focuses on reflecting tissue up to the side wall to ensure entry into the visceral compartment.

The surgeon performs hernia sac reduction on a large, thin hernia sac, with visible assistance from a bedside assistant applying gentle manual pressure over the groin. The surgeon uses a force bipolar instrument to modulate grip strength during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a strong grasp to reduce slippage and prevent tearing of the hernia sac while pulling it out of the canal. The anatomical orientation allows reflection of the spermatic vessels and the vas deferens. The surgeon transects the remaining adhesions and tissue at the end of the sac, preparing to establish the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, starting medially.

The surgeon develops the retropubic space, ensuring at least two centimeters of space between the pubis and the bladder. An areolar plane is identified, taking care not to disrupt nearby vessels to prevent potential post-operative bleeding.

The surgeon works on parietalizing the spermatic vessels and vas, carefully dissecting around these structures. Visible fat emanates from the femoral canal, which the surgeon selectively removes to avoid disrupting lymphatics and causing bleeding.

The surgeon transects and leaves a fat plug within the femoral canal, ensuring adequate parietalization for laying the mesh flat. The surgeon reflects tissue over, aiming to improve the critical view, while ensuring the mesh lays over the vessels and lateralizes them as much as possible.

The surgeon adjusts the heavyweight, extra-large mesh to ensure optimal lateral coverage over the surgical site. The mesh is then fixated to Cooper's ligament using an air knot technique, providing a secure attachment point.

The surgeon places multiple fixation points around Cooper's ligament for direct hernias, followed by a standard flap closure. The insufflation pressure is reduced, and a nine-inch V-lock suture is used for closure, despite typically using a six-inch suture.

The surgeon assesses the flatness of the mesh in the preperitoneal compartment by closing the flap, dropping the insufflation pressure with the camera inside, and then quickly re-insufflating. The pressure gradients allow the peritoneum to reflect back, confirming that the mesh is laying flat without tenting.
